turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Case study: FutureLife Financial Services
Shaun Watkins (age 44) established FutureLife Financial Services 15 years ago. Five years ago he brought
Emily Redbank (age 49) into the partnership. They are both owners and principals and hold their own
Australian financial services (AFS) licence. You are a senior financial adviser employed for the past
three years by FutureLife, with an offer for equity in the business within the next few years. With the
business having experienced exceptional growth over the past 12 months, FutureLife has decided they
need an additional adviser. Rather than hire outside the firm, they have opted to transition their young
support staff member, Josie, into an adviser role.
Shaun’s clients are predominantly self-funded retirees, with a small number who require specialised
aged care financial advice. His clients have accumulated their wealth through successful small businesses.
Shaun prefers to look after his existing clients, with any new clients referred to yourself or Emily.
Emily’s clients (age 35 to 55) are successful wealthy professionals from a mix of professional backgrounds.
Given their wealth, Emily has recommended self-managed superannuation funds to a number of these
clients.
Josie has worked in the practice in a number of support roles over the last four years while she was
completing her university degree. Josie graduated 12 months ago with an approved Bachelor of Commerce
(Financial Planning) degree. As part of her career planning, you have agreed to supervise Josie through
her professional year, and she is currently in the third quarter of her professional year.
You have 10 years experience as a financial adviser and are fully qualified to provide advice on a wide range
of strategies and products. Half of your clients followed you to FutureLife, from your previous financial
adviser role. When Shaun or Emily is on leave, you also help out by providing advice to their clients.    Scenario 1 — FutureLife clients: Les and Marg Russell
Les and Marg Russell are both in the late 60s and have been clients of the practice for many years,
mainly dealing with Shaun. They have one child, a son Robert, with whom they have a strained relationship,
and they are not on speaking terms. They do however see their only grandchild, Jennifer (age 22), who is
Robert’s daughter, on a regular basis. As Jennifer is just about to complete her university degree, Les and
Marg think it would be a good time to give Jennifer $200,000, as they have significant wealth accumulated
and would prefer to see Jennifer benefit from the funds while they are alive.
Les and Marg have already spoken to Shaun about how they believe the money should be managed and
invested. Before telling Jennifer about the gift, Les and Marg asked her what her financial goals are.
Jennifer said she wasn’t sure but would like to travel overseas or save towards a deposit for her own home.
Les and Marg insist that Jennifer receives financial advice before they gift her the money, to ensure she has
an effective financial plan in place. They think it would be better for Jennifer to deal with an adviser closer
to her own age and suggest that Josie would be suitable. Les and Marg have always found Josie to be
friendly over the years and are comfortable that you and Shaun will be mentoring Josie.
This will be Josie’s first meeting (indirectly supervised) as a provisional relevant provider. You ask Josie
about her current plan for the interview, and she provides you with the following outline:
Jennifer’s grandparents have already spoken to Shaun about how Jennifer’s gift should be
managed and invested. Les and Marg have been Shaun’s clients for a long time and have done
well financially, and I’m assuming they know what they are talking about and will be able to
provide Jennifer with the right guidance on investments too.
To save a bit of time, I’ve already emailed the FSG to Jennifer and I’ll get her to acknowledge
she has received it, when I see her.
The money will be given to Jennifer as cash. Given that Jennifer is in her early 20s, she has
a long-term investment horizon, and it sounds like high-growth investments will be an
appropriate solution for her. We’ve got some really great model portfolios of direct shares
on our approved product list, which are currently generating high returns for other clients,
that I can talk to her about. I am a bit concerned her grandparents may not be happy for her
to put all the funds just in shares, so I may need to check with them about that.
This meeting should be straightforward as we will focus just on investing the $200,000 gift
and I doubt whether Jennifer will need any insurance, given her young age. She will eventually
inherit all of her grandparents’ money anyway, and I’m sure they would look after her if
anything was to happen to her in the meantime.   IN 1200 WORDS answer the following ---- (a) Define ‘unconscious bias’ and examine how it may be influencing Josie while she is planning for the
meeting with Jennifer. (10 marks)
(b) Discuss the intent of Standard 1 and Standard 2 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics
2019 and analyse Josie’s conduct. Include in your response a discussion of whether, prior to the
meeting, Josie complies with the standards in her dealings with Jennifer.
Note to students: this question is asking you to analyse the advisers actions against compliance with
Standard 1 and Standard 2 — leading up to the appointment, rather than after the meeting has
occurred. (10 marks)
(c) Examine and discuss Josie’s compliance with the value of Fairness in the Financial Planners and
Advisers Code of Ethics 2019, if she proceeds with the interview as planned. (5 marks)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(a) Unconscious bias refers to the automatic, unconscious judgments and preferences that individuals make based on social stereotypes, personal experiences, and cultural background. These biases can unknowingly influence decision-making and interactions with others, leading to potentially unfair treatment and discrimination. In Josie's planning for the meeting with Jennifer, several instances of unconscious bias may be evident. Firstly, Josie assumes that because Les and Marg have been successful clients and have significant wealth, they will be able to provide the right guidance to Jennifer on investments. This assumption may be biased and prevent Josie from properly assessing Jennifer’s financial needs and objectives independently.

Secondly, Josie believes that high growth investments would be appropriate for Jennifer because she is in her early 20s and has a long-term investment horizon. This assumption is based on the stereotype that young people are more risk-tolerant and more likely to invest in high-growth investments. While this may be true for some individuals, it may not be the case for Jennifer, and it is essential to assess her risk tolerance and financial goals, not merely her age.

Lastly, Josie may be biased in assuming that Jennifer does not need any insurance due to her age. Josie thinks that Jennifer's grandparents will look after her if anything were to happen, and she will eventually inherit their wealth. This assumption disregards Jennifer's potential needs and personal circumstances, which could lead to inadequate protection and financial planning.

(b) Standard 1 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 states that financial advisers must act in their clients' best interests, prioritise their clients' interests over their own, and provide advice that is appropriate to their clients' circumstances. Before the meeting, Josie's conduct may not fully comply with Standard 1, as she is making assumptions about Jennifer's financial goals, risk tolerance, and insurance needs based on her age and grandparents' financial situation. By relying on these assumptions and not properly assessing Jennifer's individual needs and circumstances, Josie may not be providing advice that truly serves Jennifer's best interests.

Standard 2 of the Code of Ethics states that financial advisers must act with integrity and honesty, avoiding any misleading or deceptive conduct. Prior to the meeting, Josie's conduct may also not fully comply with Standard 2. By emailing the FSG to Jennifer and planning to obtain acknowledgement of receipt at the meeting, Josie may not be providing enough time and opportunity for Jennifer to review and understand the information contained in the FSG. To ensure compliance with Standard 2, Josie should ensure that Jennifer has ample time to review the FSG and encourage her to ask questions if she has any concerns or queries.

(c) The value of Fairness in the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 relates to treating clients with dignity, respect, and fairness, regardless of their personal characteristics or circumstances. If Josie proceeds with the interview as planned, her compliance with the value of Fairness may be compromised.

By basing her advice on Les and Marg's opinions and not conducting her own independent assessment of Jennifer's financial needs and objectives, Josie may not be treating Jennifer fairly. This could lead to Jennifer receiving advice that does not align with her personal goals and risk tolerance.

Additionally, assuming that Jennifer does not need insurance and will be taken care of by her grandparents may not be a fair consideration of her personal circumstances. It is essential for Josie to evaluate Jennifer's unique situation and provide recommendations that address her needs accurately.

In conclusion, Josie's unconscious biases and assumptions may negatively impact her compliance with the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019. To ensure adherence to the Code's standards and values, it is crucial for Josie to recognise these biases, conduct a thorough assessment of Jennifer's financial needs and circumstances, and provide advice that prioritises her best interests.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Scenario 2 — FutureLife clients: Matthew and Claudia Edwards
One of your longstanding clients, Matthew Edwards, has called to arrange an appointment for you to see
his mother Claudia (age 78). Matthew’s father passed away a little over six months ago and his estate has
now been finalised with all assets transferring into Claudia’s name. Matthew’s father John always looked
after their own finances and invested primarily through direct shares. Mathew has emailed you a portfolio
listing all the shareholdings now in Claudia’s name, as well as details of all the cash holdings. The cash and
shares add up to just over $2 million, based on the current share prices.
Mathew was going to attend the meeting with his mother but has been caught up at work and is now
unable to make it. He calls you and says to meet with his mother anyway, as it is her money, and she is
quite capable of making her own decisions.
You commence your meeting with Claudia by providing her your FSG and passing on information about
your practice and the advice you can give. Claudia just nods while you speak, and you ask her if she
understands what you have outlined. Claudia replies, ‘some of it — but it doesn’t really matter, as you
always look after Matthew, and he says you will look after me’. While you are not entirely convinced that
Claudia understands everything you have said, you are confident that she has the legal capacity to make
her own decisions, so you decide to continue with your initial meeting.
You begin the fact-finding process and ask Claudia a range of questions to understand her current situation,
goals and objectives. She says that Matthew should have provided the details of the investments, and aside
from that, she just wants ‘things to continue as they were before John passed away’. John always told
Claudia they had enough money to see them through their life and had things set up to provide a regular
amount into the bank account for them to spend, and Claudia is happy for this arrangement to continue.
She admits that she has never been part of the decision making financially, mainly because she was not
interested and just let her husband deal with things. Now she is on her own, she just wants things to
continue and says that as long as Matthew agrees with what you recommend, she will be happy with that,
and she will sign whatever needs to be signed.  --- in 1300 words answer the following ---  (a) Discuss some risks that may arise from Claudia’s apparent minimal knowledge of (or experience with)
investing and overall disinterest in the financial planning process. In your response, identify and
discuss at least one ethical barrier that is possibly impacting Claudia and at least one ethical barrier
that is possibly impacting yourself (the adviser) (10 marks)
(b) Identify at least three (3) relevant standards under the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics
2019 that have arisen for consideration based on the approach or actions that Claudia has displayed,
and explain your concerns, with reference to the standards. (10 marks)
(c) Based on your meeting with Claudia, identify and describe two (2) different appropriate ethical
frameworks that could be used to deal with this client interaction/situation.
Note to students: the ethical frameworks to choose from are the Consequentialist, Duty or Virtue
frameworks. (10 marks)
Support your answers with reference to the case study and scenario facts and research.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(a) Risks associated with Claudia's minimal knowledge and disinterest in the financial planning process include:

1. Inappropriate investment decisions: Claudia's limited investment knowledge and experience might cause her to make investment decisions based on incorrect or incomplete information, resulting in suboptimal investment outcomes. Her reliance on her son Matthew and the adviser may not lead her to the best decisions for her own circumstances.

2. Exploitation or manipulation: Claudia's disinterest in financial matters makes her vulnerable to potential misconduct or exploitation by unscrupulous parties.

3. Lack of personal involvement: Claudia's disinterest in her finances may hinder her understanding of the implications and risks associated with the decisions she is making. This could result in unexpected negative outcomes due to a lack of oversight or regular review of her financial situation.

One ethical barrier possibly impacting Claudia is self-determination. Claudia's limited financial knowledge and lack of interest in financial matters may make it challenging for her to make informed decisions about her financial future. By relying on the advice and guidance of her son Matthew and the financial adviser, she may be unintentionally relinquishing control over her financial situation and not fostering the autonomy that she should be exercising.

Conversely, one ethical barrier affecting the adviser is the potential conflict between what is in Claudia's best interest and maintaining the trusting relationship with her son, Matthew. The adviser may feel inclined to prioritize Matthew's perspective and opinions to preserve the existing relationship, instead of ensuring Claudia's best interests are genuinely being served.

(b) Three relevant standards from the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 associated with Claudia's approach and actions include:

1. Standard 1: Financial advisers have a fundamental responsibility to prioritize the client's interest and act in the client's best interest always. Claudia's reliance on Matthew's opinions while dismissing her understanding of the advice process might make it challenging for the adviser to ensure that Claudia's best interests are primarily considered.

2. Standard 3: Financial advisers should ensure that their advice demonstrates a reasonable basis and remains appropriate for the client's individual circumstances. Claudia's desire to maintain the financial status quo, despite potential changes in her financial situation, may make it challenging for the adviser to provide appropriate and well-founded recommendations.

3. Standard 5: Financial advisers must make inquiries about the client's circumstances and objectives, document these inquiries, and apply due diligence to ensure the advice provided is suitable for the client. Claudia's reticence to engage in the financial planning process and share information about her circumstances and objectives might hinder the adviser's ability to comply with this standard.

(c) There are two ethical frameworks that could be applied to deal with this client situation:

1. Duty-based (Deontological) ethical framework: This approach focuses on the obligations and responsibilities the adviser has towards the client. By adopting a duty-based approach, the adviser would uphold their fiduciary duty to prioritize Claudia's best interests, even if she appears disinterested. The adviser should continue asking questions, gathering information, and make recommendations based on robust evidence, ensuring Claudia understands the implications of the advice.

2. Virtue ethics framework: This framework emphasizes the importance of an individual's moral character in making ethical decisions. By nurturing virtues like empathy and patience, the adviser might be better equipped to help Claudia become more comfortable and engaged in the financial planning process. Adopting a virtue ethics approach, the adviser should demonstrate patience, active listening, and genuine care for Claudia's well-being, promoting an environment where Claudia feels heard and understood.

In conclusion, despite Claudia's disinterest and reliance on others, it is critical for the adviser to follow ethical standards, engage Claudia in the financial planning process, and prioritize her best interests. The adviser should apply the duty-based or virtue ethics framework to guide their decision-making, ensuring they provide the best possible service while overcoming potential ethical barriers.

2. Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy - Virtue Ethics. Retrieved from: https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ethics-virtue/

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Scenario 3 — FutureLife clients: Kevin and Anika Leste
Emily has not been well lately and has just been diagnosed with early-stage breast cancer. Her doctors have
recommended she take at least six months off from work to concentrate on her treatment plan, which they
are in the process of finalising. As Emily will be away from the office for some time, she asks you to provide
advice for her existing clients.
Your first meeting is with Emily’s clients, Kevin and Anika Leste. You will be meeting them for the first time
and Emily has told you they are important clients because they often recommend family, friends and their
own clients (from their business) to the practice.
Kevin and Anika operate a successful bookkeeping business where they manage the accounts for many
self-employed clients. There has been a longstanding referral relationship between Emily and the Lestes,
which sees them share many clients. There remains frequent cross-referral of clients between Emily and
the Lestes; however, this arrangement has been altered due to the introduction of the Financial Planners
and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019. Where in the past, Emily simply paid them a referral fee, she now adjusts
her advice fees (up or down) to Kevin and Anika, in lieu of referral payments.
The Leste’s file shows that a Statement of Advice was issued three years ago. The client notes state that
Emily recommended the Lestes establish an SMSF, and this has now been in place for nearly three years.
The file notes also state they have rolled over superannuation from their previous superannuation funds
with AustralianSuper and Aware Super. Both funds were previously invested in the ‘balanced’ options.
As bookkeepers, the Lestes undertake a lot of the SMSF administration work themselves. As you read
through their file, it appears they have little knowledge of investing. The only assets currently in the SMSF
are a $1.4 million term deposit with the CBA and the SMSF administration account with about $60,000.
You review their goals and objectives listed in their file and note that their main goals are to build wealth
for their retirement and help their adult children.
After introducing yourself to the Lestes, you explain that as you are not fully aware of their financial
situation, and as part of the review process, it would be worthwhile revisiting their current situation,
goals and objectives. Part-way through the meeting, Kevin takes a phone call and states he will need to
leave immediately to take care of some urgent business, and Anika can update him later on what was
discussed. However, as he leaves, he says that he just wants you to invest the money in the way you think
is appropriate for them as ‘Emily does this all the time’. He adds, ‘we know how the process works,
so we don’t really need to go through all the paperwork’.  --- in 1800 words answer the following and show references at the end --- (a) Explain how Emily’s conduct would be assessed under the value of Diligence and Standard 5 of the
Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019. (10 marks)
(b) Explain three (3) issues or potential breaches of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics
2019 that may arise from Emily’s actions of adjusting advice fees as a result of cross-referrals with
Kevin and Anika. (15 marks)
(c) Discuss whether Emily’s previous advice to Kevin and Anika meets the best interests obligations
under Standard 2 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019. (5 marks)
(d) Outline the obligation Standard 12 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 places
on advisers. Given your experience in dealing with the Lestes, outline three (3) actions you could take
to comply with this obligation. (10 marks)
Support your answers with reference to the case study and scenario facts and research.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(a) Emily’s conduct regarding the value of Diligence and Standard 5 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 would be assessed based on her consistent application of skill, care, and professional judgment in her client interactions. Diligence requires a financial adviser to act promptly, thoroughly, and with a genuine commitment to their clients' best interests. Standard 5 stipulates that the adviser must make inquiries into the clients' circumstances, objectives, and preferences, and conduct investigations to assess the suitability of their advice.

In the case study, Emily demonstrated an ongoing relationship with Kevin and Anika by providing advice, completing a Statement of Advice three years ago, and maintaining communication with them. However, the information available does not provide a complete picture of her diligence in conducting inquiries, updating client information, and regularly reviewing their financial situation. An assessment of her diligence and adherence to Standard 5 would require a more in-depth analysis of her client interactions, documentation, and ongoing monitoring practices.

(b) Three issues or potential breaches of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 that may arise from Emily’s actions of adjusting advice fees due to cross-referrals with Kevin and Anika are:

1. Standard 3: Emily's actions may raise concerns about the impartiality of her advice and recommendations. By adjusting fees in response to cross-referrals, Emily may create a perception that her advice may be motivated by financial incentives rather than her clients' best interests. This situation may potentially breach Standard 3, which requires advisers to ensure their advice is based on a reasonable and impartial basis.

2. Standard 9: Adjusting advice fees in response to cross-referrals may potentially create a conflict of interest or perceived conflict of interest between Emily's professional duties and her financial incentives. According to Standard 9, advisers should not accept incentives that can reasonably be expected to compromise their professional judgment. Emily's fee adjustment practice could be seen as a source of conflict between her professional obligations and her financial interests, which might negatively impact her ability to provide unbiased advice to clients.

3. Standard 7: Adjusting advice fees due to cross-referral activity may potentially breach Standard 7, which requires advisers to provide clients with clear information about the fees they charge, the services they provide, and any remuneration or other benefits received in connection with their advice. If Emily's clients are not aware of the fee adjustments or the relationship she has with Kevin and Anika, this could be viewed as a lack of transparency and potentially lead to a breach of this standard.

(c) Emily’s previous advice to Kevin and Anika regarding the establishment of an SMSF would be assessed against the best interests obligations under Standard 2 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019. Standard 2 requires advisers to act with honesty and integrity, ensuring that their actions and advice do not mislead clients or lead to adverse consequences for them.

The available information suggests that Emily recommended the SMSF after considering Kevin and Anika's goals of building wealth for their retirement and helping their adult children. In recommending the SMSF, Emily may have believed that it was the best strategy to achieve these goals. However, it is difficult to determine whether Emily's advice fully met the best interests obligations of Standard 2 without a comprehensive review of her advice process, including the inquiries she made into Kevin and Anika's circumstances and objectives, her thorough evaluation of alternatives, and her communication with the clients about the risks and benefits associated with her recommendation.

(d) Standard 12 of the Financial Planners and Advisers Code of Ethics 2019 places an obligation on advisers to report actual or suspected breaches of the Code to their Licensee. This helps ensure that financial professionals maintain the highest ethical standards and that any issues are addressed promptly and appropriately, protecting both the clients and the integrity of the profession.

Given your experience in dealing with the Lestes, three actions you could take to comply with this obligation include:

1. Documenting and reviewing the case: Carefully document your observations, concerns, and any evidence of potential breaches connected to Emily’s actions or advice. This may include fee adjustments, communication records, or inconsistencies in the advice provided to the Lestes.

2. Consulting with your Licensee: Discuss your concerns and observations with your Licensee, following your organization's established policies and procedures for reporting potential breaches. Provide them with the relevant information and documentation that supports your concerns, and engage in an open and professional dialogue about the case.

3. Cooperating with any follow-up actions or investigations: Once you have reported the potential breaches to your Licensee, cooperate with any necessary follow-up actions or investigations. This may include providing additional information, participating in interviews, or assisting with remedial actions taken by your Licensee.

References:

1. Financial Adviser Standards and Ethics Authority (FASEA) Code of Ethics Guidance - 2019. Retrieved from: https://fpa.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/FASEA-Code-of-Ethics-Guidance-1119.pdf
